network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as a highly convergent prebiotic product arising from ten literature-grounded routes distributed across hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical/prebiotic settings. The network is centered on five hub molecules—HCN, formaldehyde, aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylate, and glycine—linking atmospheric/surface cyanide chemistry, hydrothermal CO2 fixation, cyanosulfidic photochemistry, HCN polymer hydrolysis, Fischer-Tropsch-type catalysis, Bucherer-Bergs conversion, and mineral-mediated reductive amination. Hydrothermal environments generate C1-C3 feedstocks such as formate, formaldehyde precursors, and glyoxylate/pyruvate analog pools; surface environments concentrate HCN, drive Strecker and photochemical nitrile chemistry, and enable polymer hydrolysis; biochemical/prebiotic environments complete hydrolysis, transduction, and oligomerization. The design emphasizes experimentally supported convergence, realistic mineral agents for all hydrothermal and surface reactions, and cross-environment transfer of intermediates into glycine synthesis and early peptide assembly.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to C1 products

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11; CO2/H2 fixation producing formate, methanol and trace formaldehyde-equivalent C1 pools

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed hydrogenation of CO2 on iron-sulfur/iron-oxide surfaces generates reduced C1 species that feed into formaldehyde chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 reduction under alkaline hydrothermal conditions to formate, methanol, acetate, and pyruvate at 100 degrees C; formaldehyde is treated here as a connected reduced C1 feedstock necessary to bridge hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface Strecker chemistry, consistent with the environmental flow summarized in the review. This connection is mildly inferential but constrained by the documented hydrothermal generation of reduced C1 species (Preiner et al., 2020; Wimmer et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Surface atmospheric/photochemical HCN generation and concentration

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 photocatalysis with sulfide mineral assisted redox chemistry
conditions.other: CO-containing atmosphere with lightning/UV; transient reducing episodes; subsequent concentration in ponds or ice eutectics

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric chemistry and surface photoredox processes convert CO/N-bearing gases to HCN, which is then trapped in surface reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: Schlesinger & Miller (1983) found CO-containing atmospheres can yield bioorganics comparably to strongly reducing atmospheres, while the review identifies atmosphere as the main source of HCN and notes TiO2-mediated photochemistry as a plausible surface redox driver. This reaction condenses atmospheric generation into the surface node required for network connectivity and mineral specification. Supported by Schlesinger & Miller (1983), Cleaves et al. (2008), and Senanayake & Idriss (2006).

reactions.name: Initial Strecker condensation to aminomethanol and methanimine

conditions.other: warm pond chemistry near pH ~8; formaldehyde and ammonia condensed prior to cyanide addition

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia adds to formaldehyde to form aminomethanol, which dehydrates to methanimine under concentration/dehydration conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The review states the Strecker mechanism begins with NH3 attack on formaldehyde to give aminomethanol followed by dehydration to methanimine; Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) mapped this pathway in explicit aqueous solution. Mineral surfaces are included here as concentrators in warm-pond settings, consistent with Bernal-style clay concentration and borate-mediated stabilization discussed in the review (Magrino et al., 2021; Bernal, 1951).

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker cyanide addition to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration on clay/phosphate-rich pond margins
conditions.other: pH about 8; formaldehyde and HCN around 1 g/L as in prebiotic Strecker/Bucherer-Bergs studies

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide nucleophilic addition to methanimine furnishes aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the dominant prebiotic route to glycine identified by Miller (1953, 1957) and mechanistically characterized by Magrino et al. (2021), who found glycinonitrile formation favorable with a rate-limiting barrier of 15 kcal/mol for key early stages. Surface mineral concentration is prebiotically plausible though the core chemistry is solution Strecker chemistry (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1957; Magrino et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycinamide

conditions.other: aqueous prebiotic pool hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydration converts aminoacetonitrile into glycinamide.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) explicitly identified glycinamide as the nitrile hydration intermediate on the way from aminoacetonitrile to glycine, with nitrile hydration requiring about 23 kcal/mol in water. This is a core literature-supported intermediate and should be preserved in the network.

reactions.name: Glycinamide hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: continued aqueous hydrolysis; highest Strecker-path barrier step

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis yields the free amino acid glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) showed the final amide hydrolysis from glycinamide to glycine has the highest barrier in the canonical Strecker sequence, about 31 kcal/mol, but the overall pathway is strongly exergonic. This is one of the principal bottlenecks in prebiotic glycine synthesis.

reactions.name: Glycolonitrile formation from formaldehyde and HCN

conditions.catalyst: borate and clay concentration in aqueous ponds
conditions.other: pH around 8; formaldehyde and HCN ~1 g/L; glycolonitrile strongly favored at low NH3

reactions.mechanism: Cyanohydrin formation between formaldehyde and HCN gives glycolonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras and colleagues (1998) found that under realistic prebiotic conditions at pH 8 and 25 degrees C, glycolonitrile is the overwhelmingly dominant product (99.9%) from HCN/HCHO systems, making it a major reservoir and branchpoint. Schwartz & Goverde (1982) also noted glycolonitrile accelerates HCN oligomerization.

reactions.name: Glycolonitrile aminative conversion to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: clay concentration with buffered phosphate-rich aqueous medium
conditions.other: low NH3 route corresponding to minor aminoacetonitrile branch in Bucherer-Bergs/Strecker mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Ammonolysis/rearrangement of the cyanohydrin branch channels a minor fraction toward aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) reported only about 0.1% aminoacetonitrile under realistic HCN/HCHO/NH3 conditions, demonstrating that aminoacetonitrile can arise from the same formaldehyde/HCN chemical space dominated by glycolonitrile. This reaction is included to represent that experimentally observed branch and create convergence on aminoacetonitrile.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs carboxylative capture of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin

conditions.other: room temperature; CO2/bicarbonate abundant

reactions.mechanism: CO2-mediated carbamylation and cyclization traps aminoacetonitrile as hydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) demonstrated that CO2 catalyzes productive conversion of aminoacetonitrile toward hydantoin under realistic prebiotic conditions. The review also cites Pulletikurti et al. (2022) for efficient hydantoin formation in bicarbonate buffer, reinforcing the prebiotic plausibility of this carboxylative capture branch.

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolytic opening of the heterocycle

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic ring opening of hydantoin yields N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs route summarized in Taillades et al. (1998) proceeds through hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine before final hydrolysis to glycine. This preserves the experimentally supported alternative to direct Strecker hydrolysis.

reactions.name: N-carbamoylglycine hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis in prebiotic pool

reactions.mechanism: Carbamoyl hydrolysis liberates glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) identified N-carbamoylglycine as the downstream intermediate between hydantoin and glycine in the prebiotic Bucherer-Bergs scenario. This route provides a literature-supported alternative to direct nitrile hydrolysis.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN and HCN polymer

conditions.other: requires concentration above about 0.01-0.1 M HCN; eutectic freezing around 249.8K can supply high local HCN concentration

reactions.mechanism: Base-assisted HCN dimerization and higher oligomerization produce DAMN and heterogeneous HCN polymers.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) showed HCN oligomerization is concentration-dependent and proceeds through oligomers including DAMN; the review notes polymerization dominates above 0.01-0.1 M HCN and is enabled by eutectic concentration. Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) confirmed hydrolysis of these polymers yields glycine abundantly.

reactions.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis after prior HCN polymer accumulation; classic Oró-style heating around 70 degrees C over extended time

reactions.mechanism: Acid/base hydrolysis of heterogeneous HCN polymers releases amino acids, with glycine typically dominant.
reactions.reasoning: Oró's early ammoniacal HCN studies and Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) showed that HCN polymer hydrolysis yields glycine as the most abundant amino acid across many conditions. This pathway bypasses canonical Strecker chemistry.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: sulfide-mineral-associated cyanosulfidic network in phosphate buffer
conditions.other: UV irradiation at about 35 degrees C; H2S as stoichiometric reductant

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox cyanosulfidic network reductively homologates HCN-derived feedstocks to amino acid nitrile precursors including aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that HCN + H2S + UV light in phosphate buffer at 35 degrees C produces aminoacetonitrile alongside nucleotide and lipid precursors in a unified network. This is a key convergent route to glycine precursor chemistry.

reactions.name: Formamide photochemistry feeding glycine

conditions.other: UV-irradiated formamide on mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photocatalysis of formamide on TiO2 produces amino acids including glycine through radical and redox pathways.
reactions.reasoning: Senanayake & Idriss (2006) showed TiO2 surfaces convert formamide under UV to biomolecules including amino acids and nucleobases with low activation barriers. The review identifies formamide photochemistry as an additional non-Strecker route to glycine-relevant products.

reactions.name: HCN hydration to formamide

conditions.other: UV-exposed wet surface

reactions.mechanism: Hydration and surface-mediated rearrangement of HCN generate formamide as a photochemical reservoir molecule.
reactions.reasoning: The review explicitly lists UV photochemistry HCN + H2O + UV -> formamide -> nucleobases and related organics as a surface reaction class. Inclusion here is necessary to connect the documented formamide photochemical route to the glycine network, with TiO2-supported plausibility from Senanayake & Idriss (2006).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal glyoxylate formation from CO2/H2 chemistry

conditions.other: serpentinizing alkaline system; glyoxylate represented as a plausible C2 oxidation-state intermediate connected to vent carbon fixation chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and C-C coupling generate small oxygenated carbonyl acids, represented here by glyoxylate as a reductive amination substrate.
reactions.reasoning: The review establishes hydrothermal serpentinization systems as efficient generators of reduced C1-C3 metabolites (Preiner et al., 2020; Beyazay et al., 2023) and specifically identifies glyoxylate as a glycine precursor in mineral-mediated routes. This reaction is a necessary bridging abstraction to connect vent carbon fixation to the experimentally demonstrated glyoxylate-to-glycine step. It is therefore somewhat generalized but anchored in the documented vent production of central metabolic intermediates and the environmental flow table (Preiner et al., 2020; Wimmer et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Ferroan brucite mediated glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate

conditions.other: serpentinite system; pH 8-9; glycine strongly partitions onto mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination/reduction couples nitrate reduction with glyoxylate amination to produce glycine on and within the mineral phase.
reactions.reasoning: A 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) demonstrated ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate + glyoxylate with up to 97% of glycine partitioned to the mineral fraction. This is one of the strongest direct mineral-catalyzed glycine syntheses in the literature and must be included.

reactions.name: ZnS-like photoreductive amination analogue from glyoxylate to glycine in surface setting

conditions.other: photochemical amination of alpha-keto/oxo acid chemistry; included as glyoxylate-glycine reversible amination analogue

reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic reductive amination of glyoxylate with ammonia produces glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) showed ZnS catalyzes reversible amination of alpha-keto acids under UV, specifically identifying glyoxylate <-> glycine as an enzyme-like photochemical transformation. The allowed surface mineral list does not include ZnS, so sulfide minerals plus TiO2 are used as the nearest permitted mineral-surface analogue while retaining the literature-grounded reaction concept; this is the least direct mineral mapping in the network and should be viewed as a constrained proxy for the documented ZnS chemistry (Wang et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type catalytic glycine synthesis from CO, H2, and NH3

conditions.catalyst: meteoritic/native iron and iron oxide surfaces
conditions.other: high-temperature FTT regime with downstream cooler-zone condensation chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Catalyst surfaces generate HCN and formaldehyde equivalents from CO/H2/NH3, followed by in situ Strecker-type conversion to glycine in coupled hot-cold zones.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) demonstrated amino acid synthesis including glycine by Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on meteoritic iron catalysts using CO, H2, and NH3. The review explicitly notes the mechanism likely proceeds through FTT generation of formaldehyde and HCN followed by Strecker chemistry in cooler zones.

reactions.name: Spark-discharge Miller-Urey route to glycine from HCN/formaldehyde/ammonia mixture

conditions.other: spark-generated atmospheric feedstocks dissolve into aqueous flask/pool; one-week discharge classically

reactions.mechanism: Atmospherically generated HCN and aldehydes dissolve into water and undergo Strecker-type chemistry to yield glycine among amino acid mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955) identified glycine as the most abundant amino acid in spark discharge experiments, and the review states the mechanism was chemical production of HCN and aldehydes in the gas phase followed by aqueous Strecker synthesis. The minerals here represent concentration after atmospheric delivery rather than the spark itself (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Ice-eutectic NH4CN pathway to glycine

conditions.catalyst: ice eutectic concentration with mineral particulates
conditions.other: frozen NH4CN solutions; long-duration storage at about -20 to -78 C; eutectic concentration of HCN near -23.4 C

reactions.mechanism: Freezing concentrates cyanide/ammonium in brines, allowing slow oligomerization and hydrolysis chemistry that yields glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) detected glycine from NH4CN solutions frozen for long periods, while Sanchez et al. (1966) and Menor-Salvan & Marin-Yaseli (2012) highlighted eutectic concentration as a solution to the HCN concentration problem. This route is distinct from warm-pond polymer hydrolysis because the concentration mechanism is cryogenic.

reactions.name: Biochemical wet-dry oligomerization of glycine to glycylglycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling; pH around 9.8 and up to about 140 C favorable for dimerization

reactions.mechanism: Dehydration-condensation couples two glycine molecules into the dipeptide glycylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Sakata et al. (2010) found glycine dimerization is optimal around pH 9.8 and 140 degrees C, while Huber & Wachtershauser (1998) and later clay studies showed amino acid oligomerization is plausible under prebiotic condensation regimes. This reaction is included to show glycine feeding forward into biochemical assembly.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and ammonia condense to aminomethanol/methanimine, cyanide adds to form aminoacetonitrile, and stepwise hydrolysis yields glycine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile branch into Strecker chemistry
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and HCN first form glycolonitrile, which with ammonia feeds the minor aminoacetonitrile branch and then hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2 as hydantoin, then hydrolyzed through N-carbamoylglycine to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN/poly(HCN), whose hydrolysis releases glycine as the dominant amino acid.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route
pathways.description: UV-driven HCN-H2S chemistry generates aminoacetonitrile directly, followed by canonical hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Formamide photocatalytic route
pathways.description: HCN hydrates to formamide, and UV/TiO2 photochemistry yields glycine.

pathways.name: Ferroan brucite glyoxylate-nitrate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation supplies glyoxylate-like carbonyl acids, and ferroan brucite mediates direct glycine synthesis from glyoxylate plus nitrate.

pathways.name: Surface photoreductive glyoxylate amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glyoxylate is transferred to surface settings, where UV-driven sulfide-mineral photochemistry aminates it to glycine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type direct route
pathways.description: High-temperature catalytic CO/H2/NH3 chemistry on iron surfaces yields glycine through coupled FTT and Strecker-like chemistry.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey atmospheric discharge route
pathways.description: Spark-generated HCN and formaldehyde dissolve into aqueous surface reservoirs and produce glycine through mixed Strecker chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent canonical Strecker pathway and the strongest literature-aligned route in the network. The aminomethanol/methanimine to aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide to glycine sequence closely matches Miller, Miller, and Magrino et al. This is chemically sound, convergent with the HCN/formaldehyde/ammonia hubs, and plausibly transitions from surface condensation to later hydrolysis.
suggestions: Explicitly state hydrolysis conditions for nitrile-to-amide and amide-to-acid steps, since these are known kinetic bottlenecks.
Include mineral or pH assistance for surface condensation if available, though not strictly necessary for plausibility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Very strong branch into Strecker chemistry via glycolonitrile, which is well supported by prebiotic cyanohydrin chemistry and aligns with the literature emphasis on glycolonitrile as a key hub. The route is chemically plausible and reinforces network convergence.
suggestions: Clarify whether aminative conversion of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile proceeds via aminonitrile exchange or imine-equivalent chemistry under the stated conditions.
Specify concentration regime because glycolonitrile formation is favored under realistic low-ammonia settings.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good Bucherer-Bergs-like branch and strongly aligned with Taillades/Commeyras-style hydantoin capture logic. The major issue is that the classic Bucherer-Bergs chemistry generally requires cyanide/ammonium carbonate or bicarbonate context beyond simple CO2 addition, so rxn_009 is somewhat underspecified. Downstream hydrolysis to carbamoylglycine and glycine is reasonable.
suggestions: Add bicarbonate/carbonate or more explicit carbamylation conditions to improve fidelity to Bucherer-Bergs chemistry.
Indicate whether hydantoin formation is CO2-catalyzed or bicarbonate-mediated under room-temperature surface conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Plausible and literature-supported route based on HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis, with glycine often a major hydrolysis product. The main weakness is that polymer hydrolysis gives mixtures rather than a targeted glycine output, so the pathway is valid but less specific than Strecker chemistry. HCN concentration requirements are a notable environmental constraint.
suggestions: State eutectic freezing or concentrated drying conditions to address the known HCN polymerization threshold.
Represent mixed amino acid outputs from polymer hydrolysis rather than glycine alone if strict stoichiometric realism is desired.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cyanosulfidic branch is broadly aligned with Patel et al. and correctly recognizes aminoacetonitrile as an expected product. However, HCN generation from CO and NH3 in rxn_002 is oversimplified and not well matched to the atmospheric photochemical literature; the key supported step is the HCN/H2S/UV network, not this precursor generation step as written.
suggestions: Revise HCN source to atmospheric lightning/UV chemistry in CO-containing or transiently reducing atmospheres rather than direct CO + NH3 conversion.
Include phosphate-buffered UV photoredox context and the known role of metal sulfides or copper photoredox if aiming for tighter Patel et al. alignment.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN hydration to formamide is plausible, but direct formamide-to-glycine as a single step is too compressed and weakly specified. The literature supports UV/TiO2 formamide chemistry producing complex mixtures including amino acids under some conditions, but not a clean, direct glycine node with high confidence.
suggestions: Expand the route to include identified formamide photoproduct intermediates rather than a direct one-step glycine conversion.
Specify TiO2/UV conditions and mixed-product nature to improve literature fidelity.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The ferroan brucite nitrate-plus-glyoxylate step is strongly literature-aligned and one of the best hydrothermal glycine routes in the network. The weakness is upstream glyoxylate generation from CO2/H2, which is not nearly as established as hydrothermal formate/acetate/pyruvate production. Thus the pathway is good overall but relies on a more speculative feeder step.
suggestions: Differentiate clearly between experimentally demonstrated ferroan brucite glycine synthesis and the more speculative hydrothermal sourcing of glyoxylate.
Consider adding glyoxylate generation through better-supported oxidation or network transformation steps if available.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The glyoxylate-to-glycine photoreductive amination concept is supported by ZnS-mediated literature analogues, though the network notes it only as a ZnS-like analogue and not with a fully canonical listed mineral set. As with P7, the main issue is the speculative provenance of glyoxylate from hydrothermal CO2/H2 chemistry.
suggestions: State the actual photocatalyst explicitly and reconcile it with the required surface mineral list.
Tighten the provenance of glyoxylate or provide an alternative source from surface photochemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This direct Fischer-Tropsch-type route is overstated. Literature more strongly supports FTT generation of intermediates such as HCN and formaldehyde on catalysts, followed by downstream Strecker chemistry in cooler regions, rather than a single-step direct conversion of CO + H2 + NH3 to glycine. The pathway captures the spirit of FTT involvement but not its best-supported mechanistic form.
suggestions: Replace the direct one-step glycine output with FTT production of HCN/formaldehyde or other C1 intermediates feeding Strecker synthesis.
Specify catalyst identity and temperature window more carefully to avoid glycine thermal destruction at high temperature.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is literature-grounded in the broad Miller-Urey sense, but the single reaction node compresses multiple atmospheric and aqueous steps into one. It is acceptable as a summary route, yet less rigorous than explicitly representing spark production of HCN/aldehydes followed by Strecker chemistry in solution.
suggestions: Split atmospheric discharge generation of precursors from aqueous Strecker conversion for better mechanistic resolution.
Specify that glycine is formed from post-discharge aqueous chemistry rather than directly in the spark itself.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong, well-connected synthesis network with excellent topology, clear hub structure, and substantial convergence onto glycine. It satisfies the major graph requirements: sufficient nodes, no obvious isolated subgraphs, multiple independent routes to shared intermediates, and strong Hydrothermal -> Surface -> Biochemical interconnection. The hub set is appropriate, especially HCN, formaldehyde, aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylate, and glycine, and convergence is high. Literature alignment is generally good, especially for the canonical Strecker route, glycolonitrile branch, Bucherer-Bergs logic, HCN polymer hydrolysis, cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile generation, and ferroan brucite glyoxylate chemistry. The main weaknesses are over-compression and occasional overclaiming in several reactions: direct FTT synthesis to glycine is not well supported as written; direct formamide-to-glycine is too simplified; hydrothermal glyoxylate generation from CO2/H2 is speculative relative to better-supported formate/acetate/pyruvate production; and HCN generation from CO + NH3 is underspecified. A secondary issue is mineral-agent compliance: the summary states minerals are specified, but the presented network excerpt does not list them explicitly reaction-by-reaction, so strict schema compliance cannot be fully verified here. Despite these issues, the network is viable and mostly literature-grounded, with the strongest support concentrated in the Strecker-centered branches.
